title: AP World History: Modern SAQ Practice Exam 04

questions.prompt:
Source 1: Historian Elena Marques, “Fortresses and Violence in the Early Modern Indian Ocean,” modern historical interpretation, 2020.
“Portuguese expansion in the Indian Ocean marked a significant break from earlier commercial patterns. Armed ships, fortified ports, and cartaz pass systems inserted European military power into maritime trade. Even when Portuguese officials could not control all commerce, they changed the political risks of oceanic exchange.”
Source 2: Historian Arjun Sen, “Asian Merchants and Maritime Continuity,” modern historical interpretation, 2021.
“The arrival of the Portuguese did not erase older Indian Ocean commercial systems. Gujarati, Arab, Malay, Persian, and Chinese merchants continued to dominate many routes because they possessed local knowledge, credit networks, monsoon expertise, and long-standing commercial relationships.”
(a) Identify one difference between the two historians’ interpretations of Portuguese activity in the Indian Ocean.
(b) Describe one specific historical development that could be used to support one of the interpretations.
(c) Explain one way Indian Ocean trade during c. 1450–1750 was similar to or different from Atlantic trade during the same period.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a clear difference between the interpretations, such as Source 1 emphasizing disruption through Portuguese military power while Source 2 emphasizes continuity of Asian merchant networks.
(b) 1 point: Describes a relevant development, such as Portuguese seizure of ports, construction of forts, the cartaz pass system, armed trading ships, continued Gujarati or Arab merchant activity, monsoon-based trade, or continued exchange through Muslim, Hindu, and Chinese merchants.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid similarity or difference with Atlantic trade, such as greater European settler colonization and plantation slavery in the Atlantic, continued Asian merchant dominance in much of the Indian Ocean, the role of maritime empires, or the use of coerced labor and silver in Atlantic systems.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should show that Portuguese entry into the Indian Ocean both changed and failed to fully replace existing systems. The best responses use specific evidence rather than simply saying “Europeans controlled trade.” For part (c), strong answers compare systems of maritime commerce, political control, labor, or merchant participation in the Indian Ocean and Atlantic worlds.

questions.prompt:
Source: Open letter from a workers’ committee in Gdańsk, Poland, to employees in state factories, 1981. This excerpt is original but modeled on labor statements from communist Eastern Europe.
“We are told that the state speaks in the name of workers, yet workers are not permitted to speak freely in their own workplaces. A union that depends on the party cannot defend those who stand at the machines, repair the ships, and wait in lines for bread. We do not call for foreign rule, nor do we reject social justice. We ask for honest representation, the right to publish our views, and negotiations conducted without threats.”
(a) Identify the likely purpose of the open letter.
(b) Explain how the source reflects one challenge faced by communist governments during the Cold War.
(c) Explain one limitation of the source as evidence for the views of all people living under communist governments in Eastern Europe.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a plausible purpose, such as gaining support for an independent union, criticizing party-controlled labor organizations, demanding free expression, pressuring authorities to negotiate, or mobilizing factory workers.
(b) 1 point: Explains a relevant Cold War-era challenge, such as shortages, limits on political expression, tension between communist ideology and worker dissatisfaction, opposition movements, legitimacy problems, or state control over unions and media.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid limitation, such as the letter representing organized workers rather than party officials, rural populations, military leaders, intellectuals, women, or all citizens; or noting that an opposition document may emphasize grievances while downplaying support for the regime.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should use the source’s point of view and purpose. The workers’ committee challenges the state’s claim to represent labor, but it frames its demands in terms of representation and social justice rather than support for foreign domination. Strong answers should connect the source to broader Cold War tensions inside communist states without assuming that every Eastern European citizen held the same views.

questions.prompt:
Source: Plain-text version of a steamship company timetable chart, printed in London, 1888.
Estimated Passenger and Mail Travel Times from London
Route Approximate Travel Time in 1840 Approximate Travel Time in 1888
London to Bombay 3 to 4 months by sailing ship 18 to 21 days by steamship and canal route
London to Cape Town 8 to 10 weeks by sailing ship 20 to 24 days by steamship
London to Shanghai 4 to 5 months by sailing ship 35 to 40 days by steamship and coaling stops
London to Buenos Aires 7 to 9 weeks by sailing ship 25 to 30 days by steamship
Notes printed below the chart: “Regular steamship service depends on coaling stations, telegraph offices, canal routes, and port agreements.”
(a) Identify one technological or infrastructural development reflected in the chart.
(b) Explain one way the developments shown in the chart contributed to imperial expansion or global economic integration during c. 1750–1900.
(c) Explain one way faster transportation and communication could have affected migration or labor patterns during c. 1750–1900.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a valid development, such as steamships, canal routes, coaling stations, telegraph offices, port agreements, or more regular long-distance transportation.
(b) 1 point: Explains how these developments contributed to imperial expansion or economic integration, such as faster movement of officials, troops, mail, migrants, raw materials, manufactured goods, financial information, or commercial orders across long distances.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid effect on migration or labor, such as increased long-distance migration, movement of contract workers, easier circulation of merchants and officials, faster recruitment for overseas labor, or stronger links between colonial labor markets and global demand.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should connect the chart’s travel-time changes to broader nineteenth-century transformations. The key is not simply that travel became faster, but that steamships, canals, coaling stations, and telegraphs made empires and global markets easier to coordinate. Strong answers may connect transportation improvements to migration, indentured labor, settler movement, merchant mobility, or administrative control.
